paragraphs.text: Developing at a previously unthinkable pace, the digital space is now the frontier of consumer exploitation. Thankfully, the EU has been among the most active regulatory bodies in fighting for consumer rights. Nonetheless, many significant blind spots remain unregulated and new ones are opening up on a timescale of months, while democratic regulation operates on the scale of years. This necessitates broad and swift regulation to have any significant effect in this regulatory cat and mouse game. While corporations will aggressively lobby against any government infringement on their digital monopolies, consumer rights organizations and the voting public will only care for broad and swift regulation, as their attention and money are limited. If you want the public behind you, you have to think and act big and your voters will support you in your fight for their rights. As it stands now, the digital fairness act is a valuable step in the right direction. In order to counteract industry lobbying against it, attaching a few popular measures to it will ensure enthusiastic support by EU consumers. The most obvious addition concerns the 'Stop Destroying Videogames' initiative. Having recently reached over 1.4 million signatures and created significant support in online spaces, this topic is incredibly important to a lot of citizens. 'Buying' a videogame, which can be rendered inoperable by the publisher at any time for any reason defeats the purpose of a purchase entirely. This is especially egregious if the game is run entirely on the local computer, only requiring a sign in with the server to confirm that it has been 'bought' legally. Robbing the customer of a piece of entertainment and art only to sell a new (and sadly often inferior) one, cannot remain standard practice in an industry where many children make their first larger purchases. With the enormous political momentum behind the initiative, the time has come to aim higher. This same problem does not only concern videogames, but almost any other digital good one can 'buy'. 'Purchasing' a movie or piece of music on a streaming service does not grant permanent ownership in the same way a CD or Blu-ray would. When licencing agreements between streaming service and publisher ended, many 'purchased' pieces of media were taken from the consumer without compensation, defeating the purpose of the 'purchase'. This applies for software as well and has fueled a resurgence of online piracy under the motto 'If buying isn't owning, piracy isn't stealing'. Having been an existential threat to the music industry two decades earlier and to the movie industry right now, encouraging piracy through business malpractice cannot be in anyone's interest. I am hopeful that the prospect of fair, legal compensation for artists and programmers instead of piracy is in the interest of said companies and a compromise can be reached. The digital fairness act also aims to combat 'addictive design of digital products'. This should definitely include the practice of so-called 'lootboxes' in video games. Right now, this is one of the main revenue sources of some of the largest games on the market. For example, the EA Sports FC series (formerly FIFA) can now be considered a casino with a football game attached. Although, it is not technically gambling, since there is no real monetary payout, which makes it even worse, an unregulated casino where you can only lose 100%. Though lacking sufficient enforcement, the belgian national loot box ban can serve as a great blueprint. Since a overwhelming number of EA Sports FC players are children, it is imperative to act swiftly on this matter. Lastly, while perhaps being on the edges of the digital fairness act, I urge you to consider the frequent abuse of location data in this or future legislation. When most new (especially electric) cars you can buy nowadays track and sell your every movement and Strava exposed secret service agents, none of us are safe.
